body:
Kundalini and the Autonomic Nervous System: A Plain-English Explanation

You're here, aren't you? Probably staring at your screen at some ungodly hour, feeling like your body's betraying you, your mind's a runaway train, and nobody - nobody - gets it. I know that feeling. I've been there. The cold sweats, the tremors, the buzzing, the insane energy, the fear that you're losing your damn mind. It's terrifying, and it feels utterly isolating. You're not alone, though. What you're likely experiencing, this wild ride, it's often tied to something called Kundalini, and how it's messing with your autonomic nervous system.

Look, when I say Kundalini, I'm not talking about some woo-woo New Age fantasy. I'm talking about a raw, biological process that can get activated. It's energy, pure and simple, and it can hit you like a freight train. And when it does, it doesn't just mess with your head; it messes with your body, big time. That's where your autonomic nervous system (ANS) comes in. It's the silent boss of your body, running everything you don't consciously think about - your heart rate, your breathing, your digestion, your stress response. And when Kundalini gets going, it throws a wrench right into that system.

I'm not here to sugarcoat it. This isn't a walk in the park. It's tough. It's confusing. But understanding what's happening can make a huge difference. It can give you a handle, even a tiny one, on the chaos. So let's talk about it, plain and simple, no BS.

The Autonomic Nervous System: Your Body's Unsung Hero (and Sometimes Villain)

Think of your ANS as two main branches, like two sides of a coin, constantly working to keep you balanced. You've got your sympathetic nervous system (SNS) and your parasympathetic nervous system (PNS). They're always on, always doing their thing, usually without you even noticing. Until Kundalini decides to turn the volume up to eleven, that is.

The Sympathetic Nervous System: Fight, Flight, or Freeze

This is your gas pedal. It's what kicks in when you're in danger, real or perceived. It gets you ready to fight, run, or freeze solid. Your heart pounds, your breath gets shallow and fast, your muscles tense up, your digestion slows down. You get a surge of adrenaline, cortisol - all those stress hormones. It's a lifesaver if a tiger's chasing you. Not so much when you're just trying to sleep at 3 AM and your body thinks it's being chased.

When Kundalini activates, it often overstimulates the SNS. You feel wired, anxious, on edge. Your body might tremble, you might sweat buckets, your heart might race for no apparent reason. You might get that weird buzzing, vibrating feeling. That's your SNS in overdrive. It's like your internal alarm system is stuck on "CRITICAL THREAT" all the time. Right? You know what I mean? It's exhausting, isn't it? It feels like you're constantly running a marathon even when you're lying still.

I've been there, lying in bed, heart hammering, convinced I was having a heart attack. I'd check my pulse every five minutes. The doctors would say I was fine, "just anxiety." But it wasn't "just anxiety." It was my body reacting to an immense energetic surge it wasn't prepared for. It was my sympathetic nervous system screaming, "DANGER!" even though there was no danger outside of my own skin.

The Parasympathetic Nervous System: Rest and Digest

This is your brake pedal. It's what calms you down, helps you relax, digest food, repair your body, and get some sleep. Your heart rate slows, your breathing deepens, your muscles relax. It's all about "rest and digest." It brings you back to a state of balance after the SNS has done its job. Ideally, these two systems work in harmony, like a well-oiled machine, constantly adjusting to your environment.

But here's the thing: when Kundalini is really active, especially early on, the PNS can get completely overwhelmed. Or, sometimes, it can swing violently between the two. You might have periods of intense SNS activation - panic, racing heart - followed by a sudden, inexplicable crash into PNS dominance where you feel utterly drained, exhausted, and want to sleep for days. It's like your body is trying to compensate, but it's doing it in extreme, jerky movements instead of smooth transitions. It's a rollercoaster, and you didn't even buy a ticket.

Sometimes, you might get weird PNS symptoms too. Intense, sudden fatigue that feels like you've been hit by a truck. Digestive issues - weird gurgling, sudden bouts of diarrhea or constipation. Your body is just trying to make sense of the overwhelming input, trying to find a new equilibrium. It's a messy process, believe me.

Kundalini: The Energy That Shakes Your System

So, what is Kundalini in all this? In simple terms, it's a powerful life force energy, often described as coiled at the base of your spine. When it "awakens" or "rises," it's not always a gentle, blissful experience like some spiritual texts make it sound. For many, like you and me, it's a shock to the system. It's like plugging a massive power generator into a house wired for a few light bulbs. The circuits blow. Your ANS, those delicate wires, they get overloaded.

This energy, when it moves, can create intense heat, vibrations, pressure, and all sorts of bizarre physical sensations. It can feel like electricity running through your body, like something moving inside you. It's not imaginary. It's real. And your ANS interprets these sensations. The SNS often sees it as a threat, hence the panic, the racing heart, the fight-or-flight response. The PNS tries to calm it down, but it's often playing catch-up.

I remember feeling like my spine was literally vibrating, like a tuning fork. It was so intense I thought my bones were going to shatter. And with that came waves of terror. My body was reacting to an internal phenomenon it didn't have a file for. So it defaulted to "extreme danger." That's what you're feeling. Your body's alarm bells are ringing because something new is happening inside you.

If you're looking for some solid accounts from others who've been through this, I can't recommend The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) enough. It's a classic for a reason. He documents so many cases that mirror what you might be going through. It helps you see you're not crazy, you're not alone. Another great one is The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link). She gets into the nuts and bolts of what's happening and offers practical ways to cope.

The Crisis: Spiritual Emergency and ANS Dysregulation

What you're in, what I was in, it's often called a "spiritual emergency." It's not a mental illness in the traditional sense, though it can mimic many symptoms of anxiety, psychosis, or depression. It's a crisis of consciousness, yes, but it manifests heavily through physical symptoms because your ANS is going haywire. Your body is trying to adapt to a higher voltage, a different operating system, and it's struggling.

This ANS dysregulation is why you feel so utterly out of control. It's why your sleep is shot, why your digestion is a mess, why you might have bizarre temperature fluctuations, or feel sudden rushes of heat or cold. Your body's internal thermostat, its regulatory systems, they're all being recalibrated, and it's a bumpy process. Does that land? Does that make some sense of the madness?

One minute you're sweating profusely, the next you're shivering. Your pupils might be dilated. You might have weird muscle spasms or twitches. All of this points to an ANS that's struggling to maintain homeostasis, that internal balance. It's constantly trying to respond to the internal energy, but it doesn't have the right tools yet. It's learning on the fly, and it's messy.

For more on spiritual emergencies, The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link) is a must-read. It really gets into the psychological and physiological aspects of these non-ordinary states. And Spiritual Emergency: When Personal Transformation Becomes a Crisis (paid link) is the classic work on the topic. These books made me feel seen, like someone finally understood what I was going through.

Coping Strategies: Taming the Wild Beast

So yeah, you're not going crazy. Your body's just trying to handle a lot. What do you do? You can't just turn it off. But you can learn to work with it, to soothe your ANS, and to help your body integrate this energy without completely freaking out.

  • Breathe. Seriously, breathe. When your SNS is in overdrive, your breathing is shallow and fast. Consciously slow it down. Breathe in slowly through your nose, hold for a few seconds, then exhale even slower through your mouth, making a gentle "hiss" sound. Do this for five, ten minutes. It directly signals your PNS to kick in. It won't stop the Kundalini, but it can dial down the panic. It's a direct line to calming your nervous system.
  • Ground Yourself. Get outside. Feel the earth under your feet. Even if it's just standing on grass for a few minutes. Focus on your physical body, your senses. What do you see, hear, smell, touch? This pulls you out of your head and into the present, which can really help when your mind is racing.
  • Gentle Movement. Forget intense workouts for a bit. Your system is already overloaded. Think gentle yoga, slow walks, tai chi. Things that connect your mind and body without pushing you into an even higher state of arousal.
  • Nourish Your Body. Eat simple, grounding foods. Lots of vegetables, whole grains. Avoid stimulants - caffeine, sugar, alcohol. Your nervous system is already overstimulated; you don't need to add fuel to that fire. Stay hydrated.
  • Rest, Rest, Rest. Sleep might be hard, I know. But prioritize rest. Even if you can't sleep, lie down in a quiet, dark room. Let your body try to recover. Your ANS needs that downtime to repair and rebalance.
  • Find Support. This is crucial. You need someone who understands, or at least someone who believes you. A therapist who knows about spiritual emergence, or a support group. Don't try to go through this alone. I tried, and it made everything worse.
  • Acceptance (as much as you can). This is hard, I know. You just want it to stop. But fighting it, resisting it, often makes the SNS activation worse. Try, just try, to acknowledge what's happening without judgment. "Okay, my heart is racing. This is Kundalini. My body is reacting." It's not about liking it, it's about not adding another layer of panic to the situation.

It's a process. It's not a switch you can just flip. Your body is undergoing a massive re-wiring. It takes time. And it's not linear. You'll have good days and really, really bad days. Don't beat yourself up for the bad days. Just keep coming back to the basics: breathe, ground, rest, move gently, eat well, seek support. That's what saved my ass, honestly.

The Long Haul: Integration and New Normal

Eventually, your ANS will start to adapt. It won't happen overnight, but it will. The extreme swings will lessen. The intensity will wax and wane, and then, slowly, gradually, it will become more manageable. You'll learn what triggers it, what soothes it. You'll develop a new relationship with your body and your energy.

This isn't just about surviving; it's about learning to live with this new level of energy, learning to integrate it. It changes you, deeply. You won't be the same person you were before. But you can come out of this stronger, more aware, and with a deeper understanding of yourself and the world. It's a brutal initiation, but there's wisdom on the other side. Hang in there. You're tougher than you think. You've got this, even when it feels like you absolutely don't.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
